FILEDESC.SOURCEDESC: Created from original research by members of the Orlando Project

AUTHORSUMMARY.P: Best known as a popular singer and actor in London from 1728 to 1769, Catherine “Kitty” Clive (née Raftor) was also the author of four plays, all comedic afterpieces that she starred in for her benefit performances. Throughout her career, she also directly addressed her fanbase in the London press and in a published pamphlet.

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: Recently uncovered archival evidence suggests that CC was born on 15 November in either 1708 or 1709, and that her parents were away from London at the time of her birth. Scholars have differed on CC's birthdate with some placing her birth on 5 November, and others indicating 15 November.

SCHOLARNOTE.P: William Chetwood, Clive's first biographer, in his General History of the Stage, placed her birth in London on 5 November 1711.

SHORTPROSE.P: CC was born to parents from presumably white, upper-middle-class families: her mother was the daughter of an eminent London leather merchant, and her father was the son of Irish Catholic landowners. The influence of her Irish-English identity can be observed in her work.AF: CC allegedly worked as a servant before starting at Drury Lane. I'm not sure if this is worth a classissue tag. img: if the source covers it I think it would be worth adding: "Despite these origins, she was said to have worked as a servant when very young." (to convey fluidity of class at this date). And n.b. I've reworded father's Jacobitism. KLH to ST: We'll add this sentence after the current round of checking. ODNB Joncus 2
CC’s mother was Elizabeth Daniell of Fish Street Hill London, whose father, with a business there, was said to be wealthy. 1-2 126
CC’s father was William Raftor, an Irish Catholic lawyer who lost his lands in Ireland when he fought for due Jacobite side after James II relinquished the throne. He later relocated to London.2

CC had an elder brother, James Raftor, who also became an actor. One sister survived and married; at least one younger sister died in infancy; there may have been other siblings who did the same. 3 248

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: While it has been widely accepted that Catherine Raftor and George Clive, a nonpractising barrister, married in early October 1733, a recently recovered marriage licence places their union at the Savoy Chapel on this date.

SHORTPROSE.P: The Savoy Chapel was known for solemnising marriages without banns being called (that is, public notice being given), and also for marriages of actors.

The couple were estranged within years of their marriage. However, the arrangement allowed CC to claim the respectability of a married woman while working in a somewhat disreputable industry. It appears that her husband did not interfere in her professional life or financial matters. 153-54

img; Sorted out structure od sexuality and sexualidentity.
Berta Joncus has argued that the union was likely a marriage of convenience for both parties, obscuring both Catherine’s and George’s homosexuality. Later in her life, rumours circulated about CC’s sapphis tastes, and some of her correspondence to other women is highly suggestive, but early in her career she made a great effort to portray herself as an irreproachable young wife. 15-16, 153-55, 348-53

There is evidence in letters of CC's sexual relationships with women. She wrote to her friend and protégée Jane Pope: I am in mind to thank you for the oysters which were excellent; but why wou’d you give the bread out of your own mouth, particularly as I know you are remarkably fond of them, that certainly is an instance that looks kinder than if you had bought them, and accordingly they taste to me so much the sweeter; for I love every morsel of your affection sent me and always eat it up myself. 353 Not only were oysters considered a powerful aphrodisiac, but Pope is described feeding them to CC by mouth in a highly erotic manner. 353
SHORTPROSE.RESEARCHNOTE: AF: Also, I'm debating about moving the marriage, sexual identitiy, and intimate relationship sections after the occupation section. I feel like education might be a better transition from family to occupation and that the information in occupation might provide useful context for understanding these relationships. At the same time, it works just fine here chronologically, so it might be best to leave it. img: I think moving it is great idea. Education is a chron jolt here, and career is more significant than marriage, KLH to ST: We'll work on this soon, as well.

SHORTPROSE.P: Little is known about CC’s childhood or education before she began acting on the London stages in 1728. In some cases, the spelling and syntax of her letters has been taken as evidence of a neglected early education. However, the vast knowledge base demonstrated in her writing and singing contradicts these speculations. 1-2, 13-14 341-42

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: As a teenager, CC started acting in London in supporting roles at Drury Lane. Her debut performance was as Bianca in Othello. 342

SHORTPROSE.P: The young actress had a facetious Turn of Humour, and infinite Spirits, with a Voice and Manner in singing Songs of Pleasantry peculiar to herself, according to William Chetwood, the prompter at the time of CC's debut. 127 By 1732, Henry Fielding started writing characters expressly for her, such as Isabel in The Old Debauchees, Kissinda in The Covent Garden Tragedy, and Lappet in The Miser.344

P.REMUNERATION: Over time, CC became one of the highest-paid and most popular performers in London in her generation. She was particularly celebrated for her comic performance and singing, earning about £300 per year depending on the success of her benefit performances.

SHORTPROSE.P: Throughout her career, CC was a fervent supporter of other women writers and performers. She mentored Jane Pope and Mary Mozeen (née Edwards), and she performed the epilogues for plays by contemporary women playwrights Elizabeth Griffith and Frances Sheridan. 13 63-64 98 69-70

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: CC’s big break came when she performed the role of Polly in John Gay’s sensationally popular musical, The Beggar’s Opera.ST: Here, should the bibcits be between chronsprose closing tag and chronstruct closing tag? Or is this okay? Just wondering if it matters in terms of consistency, becuase in some cases, the bibcits are between chronprose closing tag and chronstruct closing tag, and sometimes, like in this case, they are before both chronpose and chronstruct closing tags. KLH: Our practice is to always include the BibCit tagging between ChronProse and ChronStruct but sometimes LEAF-Writer won't allow that, so we can edit them as needed in raw XML. 35-37

SHORTPROSE.P: When The Beggar’s Opera debuted on 29 January 1728, the part of Polly, a young ingénue, was initially performed by seventeen-year-old actress Lavinia Fenton, who stirred much public sensation due to her voice and relationship off-stage with the Duke of Bolton. When Fenton left the stage to be with Bolton, CC took over the role of Polly; it was the convention of the eighteenth-century stage for performers to own parts until they retired or agreed to pass it on to someone else. But in 1736, Theophilus Cibber, then manager of Drury Lane, attempted to give his wife, actress Susannah Cibber, the role of Polly and downgrade Clive to the more minor role of Lucy. Angered, CC launched a public campaign against the Cibbers in the press, advocating for actors’ rights.ST: Here, the bibcits tag is between two <p> tags. I think that maybe LEAFwriter didn't initially allow for the bibcits to go after the text?

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: In a dramatic turn of events, CC was again allowed to perform Polly in Gay’s The Beggar’s Opera at Drury Lane (which Theophilus Cibber had taken from her for his wife Susannah), and the audience was permitted to make a final decision on who should play the part. According to a review in the London Evening Post on 1 January 1737, CC won the audience over with an impassioned speech to her fans and an astounding performance.ST: Shouldn't CC be inside the name tag in this last sentence? kLH: Yes but LEAF-Writer is being glitchy so we'll have to use raw XML. 38-39

CC gave her final performance on the London stage. That evening, she played Flora in Susanna Centlivre's comedy The Wonder, A Woman Keeps a Secret (1714) and the Fine Lady in David Garrick's Lethe (1740). 353-54

SHORTPROSE.RESEARCHNOTE: img: I'd like to add new para reading something like: Her gift for friendship extended beyond the theatre world in perhaps surprising directions: to Horace Walpole, and to Samuel Johnson, who would seek her out when visiting the green room for conversations which both enjoyed. Cite Boswell, Life ed. Hill and Powell, 4: 7. Then new heading: Retirement. KLH to ST: We'll make these edits.

SHORTPROSE.P: Following her retirement from the stage in 1769, CC moved permanently to the Twickenham countryside in a house on writer Horace Walpole’s estate; the two had been friends since the 1740s. ST: In the Twickenham settlement tag, the current attribute mentions London, which I think is correct, but the REG attribute is empty. I think according to the tag glossary, both reg and current should be filled because Twickenham is a district of London, but I'm not sure. Should I add Twickenham in reg attribute or not, since it is already in the prose? KLH: Actually, there should be an empty Settlement tag immediately after the one for Twickenham, with London entered in the REG attribute for it. ST: I cannot add another settlement tag after the one for Twickenham. LEAF-writer doesn't allow it.In a 1771 study of Twickenham country homes, playwright Jael-Henrietta Pye described her house as a little Cottage of plain Appearance with gardens laid out in excellent Taste [culminating in] a charming Retirement. 15-16 CC wrote to her friend Jane Pope describing her contented life there: I have ten times more business now than I had when I playd the Fool as you do, I have engagements every day of my life. Routs either at home or abroad every night all the nonsense of having my hair not done time enough for my parties as I used to do for my parts with the difference that I am losing money instead of getting some but I dont mind that for I am in such good health, and such fine spirits that it is impossible for any one to be happyer. 65-66

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: CC died in her seventy-fourth year.

SHORTPROSE.P: In her correspondence prior to her death, she describes herself as intermittently ill. She passed away from illness which she had called jaundice and was buried on 14 December in Twickenham. Horace Walpole was the executor of her will.
SHORTPROSE.RESEARCHNOTE: ST: ODNB confirms everything except for the name of the church. It just says a Twickenham churchyard. KLH to ST: Hmmm, quickly skim sources to see if St Mary's Church is mentioned anywhere? If not, please delete the PlaceName tag and its content, leaving the rest from Twickenham onward. ST: I deleted the placename tag content, but I'm unable to remove the tag itself. KLH: No problem, just leave this note here.

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: CC, The Case of Mrs. Clive, Submitted to the Oublick, 1744

SHORTPROSE.P: Throughout her career, CC harnessed her celebrity as an actress and singer by writing to her fans in the London periodicals. She often turned to the public when engaging in labour disputes with the all-powerful London theatrical managers, as she did when she wrote to the London Daily Post and General Advertiser during the Polly Row of 1736-37 and during the Drury Lane Rebellion of 1743-44 when she published a pamphlet. 324-25

CHRONSTRUCT.CHRONPROSE: CC published a short pamphlet titled The Case of Mrs. Clive, Submitted to the Publick to express her frustration with recent theatre mismanagement. prelims 349

P.PMOTIVES: When manager Charles Fleetwood was mistreating the actors at Drury Lane in the 1740s (a crisis in labour relations following an attempt to break the monopoly of Drury Lane and Covent Garden by adding a third licensed theatre), CC expressed her frustration directly to her fans in the form of a short pamphlet.163-64

SHORTPROSE.P: She accused Fleetwood of stealing from her and other actors, defended the salary she received for her work, and chastised Covent Garden manager John Rich for firing her without notice: I am sorry I am reduced to say any thing in favour of myself; but, as I think I merit as much as another Performer, and the Managers are so desirous to convince me of the contrary, I hope I shall be excused; especially when I declare, that at this time, I am not in the least vain of my Profession.AF: Would <relations with publisher> work in this section? img: I don't think that's quite it. How about a ThemeTopic tag on "labour relations" above? (though I don't know if Willow will accept my addition). And pEarnings on the phrase about defending her salary. KLH to ST: We'll edit in week of Oct 27.18-19 349

Her pamphlet provoked much sympathy from the public, including the Prince and Princess of Wales, who attended an impromptu benefit concert that resulted in Rich re-hiring her for the 1744-45 season at Covent Garden. 349 42

Between 1750 and 1765, CC wrote and staged four comic afterpieces at Drury Lane: The Rehearsal; or, Bayes in Petticoats (1750), Every Woman in Her Humour (1760), The Sketch of a Fine Lady’s Return from a Rout (1763), and The Faithful Irish Woman (1765). All four plays were written and produced for her benefit performances, a convention of the eighteenth-century theatrical contract in which an actor was allocated the proceeds from one evening’s performances after house expenses.

Only her first play, Bayes in Petticoats, was published. All of her plays survive in manuscript at the Huntington Library in the John Larpent Collection. Two additional farces are sometimes attributed to CC: The Island of Slaves (1761) and a lost play titled The London ’Prentice (1754), but the authorship of these two pieces remains unconfirmed. n378-79

HEADING.TITLE: The Rehearsal; or, Bayes in Petticoats

CHRONPROSE.PPERFORMANCE: CC’s first work for the stage, The Rehearsal; or, Bayes in Petticoats, had its debut at Drury Lane.

SHORTPROSE.P:
The first performance was a success and earned £240 for Clive's benefit night that year, £34 more than the year before. 18
She revived the piece more than a dozen times after its debut through 1762, both for her own benefit and for that of other minor actors. She made revisions to it in 1751, 1753, and 1762. An epilogue by David Garrick written for a performance in 1751 is preserved in the Folger Shakespeare Library.ST: the orgname tag here does not have any attributes and no link to CWRC. KLH: That's surprising. Let's leave this note here for future orgname sleuthing. ST: I added the REF but does it need a REG and STANDARD attributes too? Alao, after validating, it shows up as an error that the REF value is invalid. Also here, Bibcits follows pArchivalLocation tag. Is this okay? 322-24 399 Audiences of the 1750s would have been familiar with Bayes in Petticoats, revived year after year, and from 1753 they could purchase a copy after it was published in London and Dublin.
33 25 399-400

P.RBESTKNOWNWORK: Bayes in Petticoats is CC's best-known and only published play.

SHORTPROSE.P:
In this satirical and self-referential comedy, CC starred as a wealthy widoe, Mrs Hazard, who is directing her first play. As indicated by the title, Clive casts herself as a female Bayes, a self-important author from The Rehearsal (1671) by George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham, presenting a feminist parody of Buckingham’s famous comedy and the misogyny in the broader rehearsal play tradition. 47-53
The plot revolves around meta-theatrical self-parody, with Hazard deciding to cast Mrs. Clive in the lead role of her upcoming burletta at Drury Lane. When Mrs. Clive sends word that she will not be able to participate, Hazard is persuaded to put on Clive’s shepherdess costume and perform as Marcella in an adaptation of William Boyce’s Corydon and Miranda (1740) instead of a burletta. Numerous other characters and their players in the piece were borrowed from Clive's recent hits, such as Lethe and The Chaplet 380-81

TEXTUALFEATURES.RESEARCHNOTE: ST: I'm unable to put a period between the closing title tag and the opening bibcits tag. KLH: No problem, we'll leave this note and catch it later.

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: CC, Every Woman in Her Humour, 1760

CHRONPROSE.PPERFORMANCE: Ten years after her first play was written, CC’s next afterpiece Every Woman in Her Humour had its single performance at her benefit.

PMANUSCRIPTHISTORY.PARCHIVALLOCATION: A copy can be found in the Larpent Collection (Huntington Library) of play manuscripts.

SHORTPROSE.P: The title is a gendered inversion of Ben Jonson’s 1598 play Every Man in His Humour. This play has a more intricate plot and a larger cast of characters than Bayes in Petticoats as a party crowd gathers at Lord and Lady Byfield's country estate. CC portrays Mrs Croston, a cranky widow who harrasses Sir John Byfield about his new wife's spending and parties. At the conclusion of the play, Sir John kicks Mrs Croston out of his home. Miss Gibberish, Lady Di Clatter, and Mrs Goodfellow, a trio of pseudo-intellectual women, provide additional entertainment to the play by arguing the relative virtues of science and philosophy; these characters are likely CC's satiric take on the tired joke of learned ladies.45-46

P.RRESPONSES: Although it was only staged once in CC's lifetime, Felicity Nussbaum deems it the best of Clive's small corpus, 178 while Richard C. Frushell states that CC's reputation as a minor playwright primarily should rest on Every Woman in Her Humour. 45

HEADING.TITLE: The Sketch of a Fine Lady’s Return from a Rout

TEXTSCOPE.PLACEHOLDER: CC, The Sketch of a Fine Lady's Return from a Rout, 1763

CHRONPROSE.PPERFORMANCE: CC used her benefit night to stage The Sketch of a Fine Lady’s Return from a Rout at Drury Lane.

SCHOLARNOTE.P: A note on the Larpent manuscript says the farce would be performed at this theatre and this time if the Lord Chamberlain made no objection, and it seems he did not.

SHORTPROSE.P: It is one of the few plays written about Irish subjects and possibly the only instance of an Irish subject treated by a woman of Irish extraction during this period. It was performed at least once in Dublin for CC on 27 June 1763. 184

The play explores themes of conflict between a woman's true desires and her domestic and social obligations. It features Sir Jeremy, the recently knighted husband of Lady Jenkings, who is enraged by her waste of money; she in turn considers him dull and uninteresting. Lady Jenkings successfully recovers her losses, and she and her husband reconcile. The Sketch of a Fine Lady’s Return from a Rout is more self-referential than Every Woman in Her Humour, and it is believed that CC's reputation as a gamestress and her English-Irish heritage is alluded to through her casting as Lady Jenkings. 184 46-47

PLASTLITERARYACTIVITY.PPERFORMANCE: The final play that CC is known to have written, The Faithful Irish Woman, debuted as the afterpiece for her benefit, apparently after some delay.

SHORTPROSE.P: CC adapted The Sketch into two acts and re-titled it The Faithful Irish Woman. 184 As Nussbaum notes, Clive’s views in The Faithful Irish Woman were not without controversy. 185 Of her later three works surviving through the Larpent collection at Huntington Library, the manuscript of this play appears to be the most heavily censored. Among the emended phrases are a number of claims regarding the quality of language spoken in England and Ireland, such as Mrs O'Conner's remark that English was spoken in the purest Manner in Dublin. 7 Specific comparisons of English with Irish people and places have been made more ambiguous, and Mrs O'Connor merely suggests instead of asserting that she will shake hands on the deal by which she rescues Capt. Truman financially. 7, 21 185

The adaption follows the same storyline and cast of characters as The Sketch but CC takes on a new role as Mrs. O'Conner, a landowning Irishwoman who is the cousin of Lady Jenkings and the romantic interest of Captain Truman, an English seacaptain who eventually loses his fortune in a shipwreck. Clive parodies her own Irish ancestry by adopting a thick accent and incorporating dialogue defending Irish culture. The proud Irish-English identity exhibited by Mrs O'Connor reverses models of language, gender, and social class observed in CC's own family heritage and plots of popular Irish plays on the English stage. Near the conclusion of the afterpiece, Mrs O'Connor sings an excerpt from Air X in The Beggar's Opera: Thus safe on shore, I ask no more; / My All is in my Possession, Possession. In doing so, CC creates a theatrical space in which she can inhabit as a dispossessed Irish-English woman by integrating references to her repossession of the role of pretty Polly with Mrs O'Connor's "possession" of Irish land. 21 184-88 46-47

WORKSCITED.SOURCE: Unless otherwise noted, all information is from [InsertSourceTitle]. [I wonder if we could scrap this now?]
Baldwin, Olive, and Thelma Wilson. “Kitty Clive: Her Birth and Marriage Dates.” Theatre Notebook, vol. 74, no. 1, 2020, pp. 2–7.
Chetwood, William Rufus. A General History of the Stage: From Its Origin in Greece Down to the Present Time. W. Owen, 1749.
Clive, Catherine. Every Woman in Her Humour. The Huntington Library, John Larpent Collection, LA 174.
Clive, Catherine. The Case of Mrs. Clive: Submitted to the Publick (London: Printed for B. Dod, 1744), 18–19.

Clive, Catherine, The Faithful Irish Woman (1765), The Huntington Library, John Larpent Collection, LA 247.AF: Email aflynn04@uoguelph.ca for copy of PDF if unable to access through Alberta. Used page numbers penciled in top corner.
Clive, Catherine. The Sketch of a Fine Lady's Return from a Rout. The Huntington Library, John Larpent Collection, LA 220.
Clive. To the Author of the London Daily Post. The London Daily Post and General Advertiser, 19 November 1736, p. 1
Griffith, Elizabeth. The Platonic Wife (1765). London, 1765.

Highfill, Philip H., et al. A Biographical Dictionary of Actors, Actresses, Musicians, Dancers, Managers and Other Stage Personnel in London, 1660-1800. Southern Illinois University Press, 1973–1993.AF: Entries on "Clive, Catherine" and "Raftor, James".
Joncus, Berta. Kitty Clive, or the Fair Songster. Boydell & Brewer, 2019.

Kinservik, Matthew J. “Benefit Play Selection at Drury Lane 1729–1769: the Cases of Mrs Cibber, Mrs Clive, and Mrs Pritchard.” Theatre Notebook 50, no. 1 (1996), 18.AF: Email aflynn04@uoguelph.ca for copy of PDF if unable to access through Alberta.

Kinservik, Matthew J. “Garrick’s Unpublished Epilogue for Catherine Clive’s The Rehearsal: or, Bays in Petticoats (1750)” Études Anglaises 49, no. 3 (1996): 320-26.AF: Email aflynn04@uoguelph.ca for copy of PDF if unable to access through Alberta.
Nussbaum, Felicity. Rival Queens : Actresses, Performance, and the Eighteenth-Century British Theater. University of Pennsylvania Press, 2010.
Pye, Jael-Henrietta Pye, A Short View of the Principal Seats and Gardens in and About Twickenham (London: n.p, 1771), 15–16.
Sheridan, Frances Chamberlaine. The Dupe (1764). London, 1764.
White, Willow. Feminist comedy: The professionalization of women playwrights in London, 1750-1800. 2021. McGill University.
